text: Fluorescent rna aptamers are an interesting tool for quantifying transcription. however, when tested in a cell-free expression system, no fluorescence is detected although successfully produce fluorescent rna aptamers does occur. the article describes this problem for the  expressway™ cell-free expression system and considers that similar issues may arise with other cell-free expression systems.
The manuscript aims to proof the incompatibility of light-up aptamers with the comercial available cell-free expression system expressway.
The article investigates the compatibility of fluorescent rna aptamers, ispinach and broccoli, with an e. coli extract based expressway cell-free expression system.

The authors use pcr to amplify a dna template containing a t7 promoter, a downstream fluorophore-binding rna aptamer sequence, and a t7 terminator region. this amplicons they used in the expressway™ mini cell-free synthesis kit and supplemented dfhbi to do in vitro transcription. the reaction was measured in a spectrophotometer to detect fluorescence over time. rna purification was performed using a reliaprep™ rna miniprep system.
The authors use in vitro transcription in the cell-free system for testing the effects on the light-up aptamers.

The authors used pcr to amplify linear dna templates and purified the products with a kit. they used the expressway mini cell-free synthesis kit for their experiments. additionally, they tested a different rna aptamer transcription buffer. they measured the fluorescence with a spectrophotometer. finally, they performed an rna purification.
The authors describe issues with a common method used to quantifying transcription. no novel method is described. hence it is a method paper and the answer is 'no'.
The authors aim to better understand the compatibility of light-up aptamers and commercial cell-free systems.
The authors do not introduce a new method. instead, they test the compatibility of rna aptamers with a cell-free expression system. therefore, this is not a method paper.
